mnemonics.title: THREAD BENEFITS
mnemonics.content: M E S S (Memory Efficient - Shared resources, Enhanced Responsiveness, Simplified Communication, Speed/Parallelism)

mnemonics.title: THREAD STATES
mnemonics.content: NEW READY RUNNING BLOCKED TERMINATED - Think of it as: Newborn -> Ready to work -> Running around -> Blocked by traffic -> Terminated/Finished

acronyms.expansion: Thread Control Block - A data structure that stores all information about a thread including its ID, state, program counter, registers, and stack pointer
acronyms.usage: The OS maintains a TCB for each thread to manage thread scheduling and switching

acronyms.expansion: Portable Operating System Interface - A family of standards specifying a portable interface for UNIX-like systems
acronyms.usage: POSIX threads (pthread) provide a standardized API for thread creation and management in C programs

acronyms.expansion: POSIX Thread - A thread library API for creating and managing threads in POSIX-compliant systems
acronyms.usage: pthread_create(), pthread_join(), pthread_mutex_lock() are common pthread functions

acronyms.expansion: Inter-Process Communication - Mechanisms for processes to communicate
acronyms.usage: Threads in the same process can communicate via shared memory without IPC, but separate processes need IPC

visualTips.visualization: Draw a process as a house with rooms (address space). Threads are people moving around inside the same house, sharing the kitchen and living room but having their own bedrooms (stack). Processes are separate houses that cannot see each other's rooms.

Many-to-One: One security guard (kernel thread) managing many people (user threads). One-to-One: Each person has their own guard. Many-to-Many: Multiple guards managing multiple people with flexible assignment.
Think of a movie theater queue: NEW = just arrived, READY = in line waiting, RUNNING = inside watching movie, BLOCKED = stepped out for snacks, TERMINATED = movie finished and left.
Imagine two students (threads) sharing one notebook (CPU). They take turns writing - when one stops, the other continues from where they left off. The saved state is like bookmarking the page.

analogies.analogy: If a process is a factory, a thread is a worker in that factory. The factory (process) has shared resources (machinery, raw materials), but each worker (thread) has their own tools (stack) and does specific tasks independently.

A web browser uses multiple threads: one thread loads images, another handles user clicks, another renders the display. If image loading blocks, you can still scroll and click - the browser remains responsive.
A restaurant kitchen has a fixed number of chefs (thread pool) waiting for orders. When orders come in (tasks), available chefs pick them up. This is faster than hiring a new chef for each order.
Two people trying to update the same Google Doc at the same time - whichever saves last overwrites the other's changes. Without coordination (locking), the final document may have unexpected content.
